part_title: Section Fifteen: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability
part_short_title: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability
part_subtitle: Evaluation of IPM Performance and Economic Viability

items.text: Chapter Ten
Integrated Management of Tuta absoluta
Introduction

An integrated management programme for Tuta absoluta is not complete merely because traps have been installed, natural enemies released, or insecticides applied. The actual outcome achieved by these actions must be measured.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An integrated management programme for Tuta absoluta is not complete merely because traps have been installed, [[term:biological-control]]natural enemies[[/term]] released, or insecticides applied. The actual outcome achieved by these actions must be measured.

items.text: A programme may appear entomologically successful because larval numbers decline, yet be economically inefficient because application costs are too high. Another programme may produce a high total yield while the value of marketable produce declines because small holes occur in a large proportion of fruits.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A programme may appear entomologically successful because larval numbers decline, yet be economically inefficient because application costs are too high. Another programme may produce a high total yield while the value of marketable produce declines because small holes occur in a large proportion of fruits.

items.text: The programme should therefore be evaluated through six interrelated dimensions:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The programme should therefore be evaluated through six interrelated dimensions:

items.text: Success should not be judged from a single indicator such as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Success should not be judged from a single indicator such as:

items.text: Indicators should be integrated to provide a balanced assessment.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Indicators should be integrated to provide a balanced assessment.

items.text: Objectives of Evaluation
Evaluation aims to answer the following questions:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Evaluation aims to answer the following questions:

items: Did the programme conserve natural enemies?
Did the number of chemical applications decline?
Did the crop comply with residue limits?
Was programme cost lower than the value of losses prevented?
Which components increased cost without a clear return?
Can the programme be repeated in the following season?
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Did the number of chemical applications decline?
[[prepared]]Did the crop comply with residue limits?
[[prepared]]Was programme cost lower than the value of losses prevented?
[[prepared]]Which components increased cost without a clear return?
[[prepared]]Can the programme be repeated in the following season?

Operational Evaluation
This measures the extent to which the plan was implemented as designed, such as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This measures the extent to which the plan was implemented as designed, such as:

This measures programme effects on:

This compares:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This compares:

This measures:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This measures:

items.text: Need for a Baseline
Change cannot be assessed without knowing the previous condition.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Change cannot be assessed without knowing the previous condition.

items.text: The baseline includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The baseline includes:

items: Cases of residue exceedance or shipment rejection.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Cases of residue exceedance or shipment rejection.

items.text: The baseline is used for comparison with the results of the new programme.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The baseline is used for comparison with the results of the new programme.

items.text: Selecting an Appropriate Comparison
The programme may be compared with:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The programme may be compared with:

items: An untreated sector in a research trial.
The mean of several similar fields.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]An untreated sector in a research trial.

items.text: The comparison should be similar in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The comparison should be similar in:

items.text: Yield differences should not be attributed to the Tuta programme when sectors differ substantially in irrigation, fertility, or cultivar.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Yield differences should not be attributed to the Tuta programme when sectors differ substantially in irrigation, fertility, or cultivar.

items.text: Difference Between a Research Trial and Commercial Evaluation
Research Trial
This requires:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This requires:

items.text: Commercial Evaluation
This focuses on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This focuses on:

items.text: A non-replicated commercial evaluation cannot prove causality to the same degree as a controlled experiment, but it remains important for practical decision-making.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A non-replicated commercial evaluation cannot prove causality to the same degree as a controlled experiment, but it remains important for practical decision-making.

items.text: First: Biological Indicators
Pheromone-Trap Catch
This is used to evaluate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is used to evaluate:

items: Effects of removing an old field.
Success of mating disruption through reduced access to traps.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Success of mating disruption through reduced access to traps.

items.text: It should not be used alone to measure final efficacy because:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It should not be used alone to measure final efficacy because:

items: Catch is affected by lure strength.
Catch may decline under mating disruption without mating stopping completely.
Trap catch may increase while plant infestation remains limited.
Fertilized females may still be present despite low male catch.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Catch may decline under mating disruption without mating stopping completely.
[[prepared]]Trap catch may increase while plant infestation remains limited.
[[prepared]]Fertilized females may still be present despite low male catch.

items.text: When exposure periods differ, results should be expressed as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When exposure periods differ, results should be expressed as:

items.text: Males/trap/day
Egg Density
This is measured using:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is measured using:

items: Eggs per leaf or group of leaves.
Number of eggs on field edges compared with the interior.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Eggs per leaf or group of leaves.
[[prepared]]Number of eggs on field edges compared with the interior.

items.text: It is useful for:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is useful for:

items.text: The following should be distinguished:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be distinguished:

items.text: Egg-Parasitism Percentage
This is calculated as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is calculated as:

items.text: Egg parasitism (%) = Number of parasitized eggs ÷ Total evaluable eggs × 100
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Egg parasitism (%) = Number of parasitized eggs ÷ Total evaluable eggs × 100

items.text: Old eggshells or eggs of indeterminate status should not be included in the denominator without explanation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Old eggshells or eggs of indeterminate status should not be included in the denominator without explanation.

items.text: Successful parasitism should preferably be confirmed through:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Successful parasitism should preferably be confirmed through:

items: Presence of a characteristic exit hole.
Laboratory holding of samples where necessary.

items.text: A high parasitism percentage does not necessarily mean the programme succeeded when total egg density is extremely high, because a large absolute number of viable eggs may remain.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A high parasitism percentage does not necessarily mean the programme succeeded when total egg density is extremely high, because a large absolute number of viable eggs may remain.

items.text: Example:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Example:

items: 80% parasitism of 10 eggs/plant leaves two viable eggs.
50% parasitism of one egg/plant leaves only 0.5 egg.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]80% parasitism of 10 eggs/plant leaves two viable eggs.
[[prepared]]50% parasitism of one egg/plant leaves only 0.5 egg.

items.text: The absolute number of surviving eggs should therefore be evaluated together with the percentage.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The absolute number of surviving eggs should therefore be evaluated together with the percentage.

items.text: Active Mines
Active mines are more informative than the total number of visible mines.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Active mines are more informative than the total number of visible mines.

items.text: An active mine is usually characterized by one or more of the following:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An active mine is usually characterized by one or more of the following:

items: Increase in mine area between two visits.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Increase in mine area between two visits.

items.text: Old mines may remain visible after:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Old mines may remain visible after:

items.text: The indicator may be expressed as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The indicator may be expressed as:

items.text: Active mines/plant
Or:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Or:

items.text: Percentage of plants with active mines
Live Larvae
Larvae should be classified as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Larvae should be classified as:

items.text: Larval-age distribution helps evaluate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Larval-age distribution helps evaluate:

items.text: A high proportion of L3 and L4 after a programme intended to target hatching indicates:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A high proportion of L3 and L4 after a programme intended to target hatching indicates:

items.text: Growing-Point and Stem Infestation
The following should be recorded:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be recorded:

items: Number of plants that lost the growing point.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Number of plants that lost the growing point.

items.text: This is particularly important in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is particularly important in:

items.text: Infestation of a small number of growing points may be more damaging than a larger number of small mines in old lower leaves.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Infestation of a small number of growing points may be more damaging than a larger number of small mines in old lower leaves.

items.text: Fruit Infestation by Number
This is calculated as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is calculated as:

items.text: Fruit infestation by number (%) = Number of Tuta-infested fruits ÷ Total fruits examined × 100
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Fruit infestation by number (%) = Number of Tuta-infested fruits ÷ Total fruits examined × 100

items.text: Infested fruits include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Infested fruits include:

items: Fruits with a confirmed entry or exit hole.
Fruits rotted because of the infestation wound when the rot can be linked to Tuta.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Fruits with a confirmed entry or exit hole.
[[prepared]]Fruits rotted because of the infestation wound when the rot can be linked to Tuta.

items.text: The following should preferably be recorded separately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should preferably be recorded separately:

items.text: Fruit Infestation by Weight
This is calculated as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is calculated as:

items.text: Weight loss caused by Tuta (%) = Weight of infested and rejected fruits ÷ Total sample weight × 100
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Weight loss caused by Tuta (%) = Weight of infested and rejected fruits ÷ Total sample weight × 100

items.text: The result may differ from numerical fruit infestation when infested fruits are:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The result may differ from numerical fruit infestation when infested fruits are:

items: Completely rejected despite retaining most of their weight.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Completely rejected despite retaining most of their weight.

items.text: Both indicators should be used together.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Both indicators should be used together.

items.text: Total Yield and Marketable Yield
Total Yield
The total weight of harvested fruits, including fruits that may later be rejected.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The total weight of harvested fruits, including fruits that may later be rejected.

items.text: Marketable Yield
The weight of fruits meeting market or factory specifications.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The weight of fruits meeting market or factory specifications.

items.text: Total yield may remain high despite major economic loss when some fruits carry small holes that make them unacceptable.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Total yield may remain high despite major economic loss when some fruits carry small holes that make them unacceptable.

items.text: Therefore:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Therefore:

items.text: Marketable or processable accepted yield
Is the most important economic yield indicator, rather than total yield alone.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Is the most important economic yield indicator, rather than total yield alone.

items.text: Conversion to Accepted Yield
Calculate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Calculate:

items.text: Acceptance rate (%) = Weight of accepted fruits ÷ Total harvested fruit weight × 100
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Acceptance rate (%) = Weight of accepted fruits ÷ Total harvested fruit weight × 100

items.text: And:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]And:

items.text: Rejection rate (%) = 100 − Acceptance rate
Reasons for rejection should be identified separately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Reasons for rejection should be identified separately:

items.text: This prevents all rejected fruits from being attributed to the pest.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This prevents all rejected fruits from being attributed to the pest.

items.text: Second: Quality Indicators
Processing-Tomato Quality
Depending on factory requirements, evaluation may include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Depending on factory requirements, evaluation may include:

items: Percentage of green or unripe fruits.

items.text: Tuta absoluta should not be assumed to reduce °Brix directly in all cases. Its principal economic effects may instead occur through:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tuta absoluta should not be assumed to reduce °Brix directly in all cases. Its principal economic effects may instead occur through:

items.text: Fresh-Market Tomato Quality
Market value can be strongly reduced even by limited superficial infestation because of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Market value can be strongly reduced even by limited superficial infestation because of:

items: Possibility of a larva inside the fruit.
Loss of confidence in the consignment.
Difficulty detecting all infested fruits during grading.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Possibility of a larva inside the fruit.
[[prepared]]Difficulty detecting all infested fruits during grading.

items.text: Tolerance in the fresh market may therefore be lower than in processing tomatoes, depending on buyer specifications.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tolerance in the fresh market may therefore be lower than in processing tomatoes, depending on buyer specifications.

items.text: Crop Safety and Residues
Record:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Record:

items: Comparison with legal and commercial limits.
Number of materials not accepted by the target market.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Number of materials not accepted by the target market.

items.text: A programme is not successful if it suppresses Tuta but causes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A programme is not successful if it suppresses Tuta but causes:

items: Rejection by the factory or market.
Repeated use of a large number of active ingredients.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Repeated use of a large number of active ingredients.

items.text: Third: Natural-Enemy and Sustainability Indicators
Predator Density
This should be measured using fixed units such as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This should be measured using fixed units such as:

items: Percentage of plants carrying a predator.
Predators per defined number of leaves.

items.text: The following should be recorded separately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be recorded separately:

items.text: Presence of nymphs indicates reproduction and establishment, whereas adults alone may represent recently released individuals.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Presence of nymphs indicates reproduction and establishment, whereas adults alone may represent recently released individuals.

items.text: Larval Parasitoids
These are assessed through:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These are assessed through:

items: Host mortality resulting from host feeding.

items.text: Every dead larva should not be classified as parasitized without confirmation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Every dead larva should not be classified as parasitized without confirmation.

items.text: Natural-Enemy Retention
A simple index may be calculated as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A simple index may be calculated as:

items.text: Natural-enemy retention (%) = Natural-enemy density after intervention ÷ Density before intervention × 100
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Natural-enemy retention (%) = Natural-enemy density after intervention ÷ Density before intervention × 100

items.text: This should be interpreted cautiously because density is also affected by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This should be interpreted cautiously because density is also affected by:

items.text: It is more informative when compared with a reference sector or time series.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is more informative when compared with a reference sector or time series.

items.text: Number of Chemical Applications
Record:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Record:

items: Number of sprays targeting other pests that also exposed Tuta to the active ingredient.
Number of repetitions of each group.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Number of sprays targeting other pests that also exposed Tuta to the [[term:active-ingredient]]active ingredient[[/term]].

items.text: Compare the programme with:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Compare the programme with:

items.text: Reduced spray number should not be considered successful when fruit infestation or economic losses increase unacceptably.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Reduced spray number should not be considered successful when fruit infestation or economic losses increase unacceptably.

items.text: Pesticide-Pressure Index
A project may develop an internal index assigning points to each application according to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A project may develop an internal index assigning points to each application according to:

items.text: This index should not be treated as a legal standard, but as a tool for comparing programmes and seasons.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This index should not be treated as a legal standard, but as a tool for comparing programmes and seasons.

items.text: Insecticide-Resistance Indicators
These include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These include:

items.text: Preserving pest susceptibility can be considered a long-term economic benefit, even when its full value is not realized within one season.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Preserving pest susceptibility can be considered a long-term economic benefit, even when its full value is not realized within one season.

items.text: Fourth: Operational Indicators
Compliance with Inspection Rounds
Calculate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Calculate:

items.text: Inspection compliance (%) = Number of rounds completed on schedule ÷ Number of planned rounds × 100
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Inspection compliance (%) = Number of rounds completed on schedule ÷ Number of planned rounds × 100

items.text: Also record:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Also record:

items.text: Programme failure may result from poor implementation rather than weakness of the scientific components.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Programme failure may result from poor implementation rather than weakness of the scientific components.

items.text: Quality of Trap Data
Indicators include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Indicators include:

items: Percentage of traps with sound lures.
Percentage of readings completed on schedule.

items.text: Calculate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Calculate:

items.text: Trap-network operational efficiency (%) = Number of traps suitable for reading ÷ Total number of planned traps × 100
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Trap-network operational efficiency (%) = Number of traps suitable for reading ÷ Total number of planned traps × 100

items.text: Accuracy of Intervention Timing
The time may be measured between:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The time may be measured between:

items.text: Or between:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Or between:

items.text: The longer the delay, the greater the likelihood of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The longer the delay, the greater the likelihood of:

items.text: Spray-Application Quality
This is evaluated through:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is evaluated through:

items: Percentage of the area not adequately covered.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Percentage of the area not adequately covered.

items.text: A result should not be attributed to the active ingredient unless delivery to the target sites has been demonstrated.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A result should not be attributed to the active ingredient unless delivery to the target sites has been demonstrated.

items.text: Implementation of Residue Removal
Measure:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Measure:

items: Time between final harvest and crop termination.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Time between final harvest and crop termination.

items.text: Delayed removal indicates increased risk of the field becoming a source.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Delayed removal indicates increased risk of the field becoming a source.

items.text: Fifth: Economic Evaluation
Concept of Economic Analysis
Economic analysis determines whether the monetary value of losses prevented by the programme exceeds the cost of implementation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Economic analysis determines whether the monetary value of losses prevented by the programme exceeds the cost of implementation.

items.text: A programme may be more entomologically effective yet:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A programme may be more entomologically effective yet:

items: Difficult to apply on large areas.

items.text: Conversely, a programme with higher direct costs may be more profitable when it:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Conversely, a programme with higher direct costs may be more profitable when it:

items.text: Direct Costs
These include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These include:

items.text: Labour cost should be included even when permanent farm workers perform the work, because labour is an economic resource used by the programme.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Labour cost should be included even when permanent farm workers perform the work, because labour is an economic resource used by the programme.

items.text: Indirect Costs
These include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These include:

items: Losses imposed on neighbouring fields by unmanaged residues.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Losses imposed on neighbouring fields by unmanaged residues.

items.text: Some of these costs are difficult to quantify precisely but should not be ignored when programmes are compared.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some of these costs are difficult to quantify precisely but should not be ignored when programmes are compared.

items.text: Direct Benefits
These include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These include:

items.text: Long-Term Benefits
These include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These include:

items: Continued acceptance by the factory or market.
Prevention of the field becoming an infestation source.
Reduced pest pressure in the following season.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Continued acceptance by the factory or market.
[[prepared]]Prevention of the field becoming an infestation source.
[[prepared]]Reduced pest pressure in the following season.

items.text: These benefits are not fully expressed in a one-season calculation but are important when programme sustainability is assessed.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These benefits are not fully expressed in a one-season calculation but are important when programme sustainability is assessed.

items.text: Partial Budget Analysis
Partial Budget Analysis
This is used to compare a new programme with the standard practice.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is used to compare a new programme with the standard practice.

items.text: It consists of four elements.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It consists of four elements.

items.text: Additional Revenue
Such as the value of additional accepted yield.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Such as the value of additional accepted yield.

items.text: Reduced Costs
Such as the value of sprays that were not applied.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Such as the value of sprays that were not applied.

items.text: Additional Costs
Such as the cost of parasitoids, traps, and inspection.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Such as the cost of parasitoids, traps, and inspection.

items.text: Lost Revenue
Such as yield lost because of a slower intervention or land occupied by supporting plants.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Such as yield lost because of a slower intervention or land occupied by supporting plants.

items.text: Calculate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Calculate:

items.text: Net economic change = Additional revenue + Reduced costs − Additional costs − Lost revenue
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Net economic change = Additional revenue + Reduced costs − Additional costs − Lost revenue

items.text: A positive result indicates that the new programme is economically superior according to the components included.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A positive result indicates that the new programme is economically superior according to the components included.

items.text: Gross Revenue
Calculate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Calculate:

items.text: Gross revenue = Marketable yield × Unit price
Where price grades exist:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Where price grades exist:

items.text: Gross revenue = Sum of weight in each grade × Its price
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Gross revenue = Sum of weight in each grade × Its price

items.text: Total yield should not be multiplied by the premium-grade price when part of the crop is rejected or sold at a lower grade.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Total yield should not be multiplied by the premium-grade price when part of the crop is rejected or sold at a lower grade.

items.text: Gross Margin
Calculate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Calculate:

items.text: Gross margin = Gross revenue − Total variable costs
Total variable costs may include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Total variable costs may include:

items: Other inputs included in the comparison.

items.text: When only the Tuta programme is being evaluated, a partial budget may be used instead of recalculating all production costs.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When only the Tuta programme is being evaluated, a partial budget may be used instead of recalculating all production costs.

items.text: Benefit–Cost Ratio
Benefit–Cost Ratio
This can be calculated using more than one definition, so the equation used should always be stated.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This can be calculated using more than one definition, so the equation used should always be stated.

items.text: Overall Ratio
BCR = Total benefits ÷ Total costs
Incremental Ratio for an IPM Programme
Incremental BCR = Value of avoided losses or additional revenue ÷ Additional programme cost
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Incremental BCR = Value of avoided losses or additional revenue ÷ Additional programme cost

items.text: Interpretation:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Interpretation:

items: Less than 1: calculated benefits are lower than costs.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Less than 1: calculated benefits are lower than costs.

items.text: Results from two studies should not be compared unless both use the same definition.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Results from two studies should not be compared unless both use the same definition.

items.text: Return on Investment
Return on Investment
Calculate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Calculate:

items.text: ROI (%) = Net return generated by the programme ÷ Programme cost × 100
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]ROI (%) = Net return generated by the programme ÷ Programme cost × 100

items.text: Or in incremental comparison:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Or in incremental comparison:

items.text: Incremental ROI (%) = Incremental net benefit ÷ Incremental cost × 100
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Incremental ROI (%) = Incremental net benefit ÷ Incremental cost × 100

items.text: The calculation should state whether programme cost includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The calculation should state whether programme cost includes:

items.text: Cost of Protecting One Accepted Tonne
Calculate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Calculate:

items.text: Cost of protecting one accepted tonne = Total cost of the Tuta programme ÷ Tonnes of accepted yield
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cost of protecting one accepted tonne = Total cost of the Tuta programme ÷ Tonnes of accepted yield

items.text: This is useful for comparing:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is useful for comparing:

items.text: A programme with a lower cost per feddan may have a higher cost per tonne when accepted yield is low.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A programme with a lower cost per feddan may have a higher cost per tonne when accepted yield is low.

items.text: Cost of Preventing One Kilogram of Loss
Calculate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Calculate:

items.text: Cost of preventing one kilogram of loss = Additional programme cost ÷ Additional accepted yield
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cost of preventing one kilogram of loss = Additional programme cost ÷ Additional accepted yield

items.text: The result should be compared with the selling price per kilogram.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The result should be compared with the selling price per kilogram.

items.text: When the cost of protecting one kilogram exceeds its market value, the programme needs adjustment unless it provides other important benefits such as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When the cost of protecting one kilogram exceeds its market value, the programme needs adjustment unless it provides other important benefits such as:

items.text: Break-Even Point
The break-even point is the additional yield required to recover programme cost.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The break-even point is the additional yield required to recover programme cost.

items.text: Calculate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Calculate:

items.text: Additional yield required for break-even = Additional programme cost ÷ Selling price per unit of yield
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Additional yield required for break-even = Additional programme cost ÷ Selling price per unit of yield

items.text: General example:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]General example:

items.text: If the additional programme cost is 3,000 monetary units and the price per tonne is 3,000 monetary units:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]If the additional programme cost is 3,000 monetary units and the price per tonne is 3,000 monetary units:

items.text: Required break-even yield = One additional marketable tonne
Net price should be used after subtracting additional harvesting and packaging costs associated with the extra yield.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Net price should be used after subtracting additional harvesting and packaging costs associated with the extra yield.

items.text: Marginal Rate of Return
When comparing two programmes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When comparing two programmes:

items.text: Marginal rate of return (%) = Increase in net benefit ÷ Increase in cost × 100
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Marginal rate of return (%) = Increase in net benefit ÷ Increase in cost × 100

items.text: This determines whether the additional cost of the more intensive programme is justified by the increased return.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This determines whether the additional cost of the more intensive programme is justified by the increased return.

items.text: A programme does not become economically preferable merely because it produces the highest yield if the additional income is lower than the additional cost.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A programme does not become economically preferable merely because it produces the highest yield if the additional income is lower than the additional cost.

items.text: Sixth: Economic Damage and Thresholds
Economic Injury Level Equation
The general equation is:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The general equation is:

items.text: EIL = C ÷ (V × I × D × K)
Where:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Where:

items: C = Cost of the control action per unit area.
V = Value per unit of crop.
I = Injury caused by one pest individual.
D = Yield loss resulting from one unit of injury.
K = Proportionate reduction expected from the control measure.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]C = Cost of the control action per unit area.
[[prepared]]I = Injury caused by one pest individual.
[[prepared]]D = Yield loss resulting from one unit of injury.
[[prepared]]K = Proportionate reduction expected from the control measure.

items.text: In studies directly relating pest density to crop loss using one regression slope, the equation can be simplified to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In studies directly relating pest density to crop loss using one regression slope, the equation can be simplified to:

items.text: EIL = C ÷ (V × b × K)
Where b is the loss caused by each unit increase in pest density.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Where b is the loss caused by each unit increase in pest density.

items.text: The units used should remain consistent.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The units used should remain consistent.

items.text: Economic Threshold
The economic threshold, or intervention threshold, is the pest level at which action should begin before the population reaches the economic injury level.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The [[term:economic-threshold]]economic threshold[[/term]], or intervention threshold, is the pest level at which action should begin before the population reaches the economic injury level.

items.text: The threshold is lower than the EIL because it accounts for:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The threshold is lower than the EIL because it accounts for:

items: Time required to detect the problem.
Speed of action of the control measure.
Proximity of the crop to a sensitive stage.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Speed of action of the control measure.
[[prepared]]Proximity of the crop to a sensitive stage.

items.text: There is no fixed percentage of the EIL that the threshold must equal in every situation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]There is no fixed percentage of the EIL that the threshold must equal in every situation.

items.text: Dynamic Nature of Thresholds
The threshold changes with:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The threshold changes with:

items.text: An increase in crop price generally lowers the amount of damage that can be tolerated, while an increase in control cost may raise the EIL.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An increase in crop price generally lowers the amount of damage that can be tolerated, while an increase in control cost may raise the EIL.

items.text: Old or foreign thresholds should therefore not be used automatically.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Old or foreign thresholds should therefore not be used automatically.

items.text: Published Threshold Examples
Studies have produced different values, including:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Studies have produced different values, including:

items: Approximately 3 larvae/plant as the economic injury level and 2.25 larvae/plant as the intervention threshold in an Ethiopian greenhouse trial.
Approximately 3.82 larvae/plant as the economic injury level and 2.87 larvae/plant as the intervention threshold in an Ethiopian open-field trial.
Approximately 5.7–6.3 larvae/plant in a two-season open-field trial in western Iran.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Approximately 3 larvae/plant as the economic injury level and 2.25 larvae/plant as the intervention threshold in an Ethiopian greenhouse trial.
[[prepared]]Approximately 3.82 larvae/plant as the economic injury level and 2.87 larvae/plant as the intervention threshold in an Ethiopian open-field trial.
[[prepared]]Approximately 5.7–6.3 larvae/plant in a two-season open-field trial in western Iran.

items.text: The differences do not necessarily represent contradiction because they reflect differences in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The differences do not necessarily represent contradiction because they reflect differences in:

items.text: None of these values should be transferred directly to Egyptian tomatoes without local calibration.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]None of these values should be transferred directly to Egyptian tomatoes without local calibration.

items.text: Selecting the Damage Indicator
Pest density may be related to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pest density may be related to:

items.text: For Tuta absoluta:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]For Tuta absoluta:

items.text: Number or percentage of infested fruits
May be more economically informative than total yield loss because a fruit may retain its weight, yet a small hole may make it unmarketable.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]May be more economically informative than total yield loss because a fruit may retain its weight, yet a small hole may make it unmarketable.

items.text: Threshold Differences by Crop Stage
Early Vegetative Growth
The plant can compensate for limited leaf injury when growing points and fruits remain intact.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The plant can compensate for limited leaf injury when growing points and fruits remain intact.

items.text: Flowering and Fruit Set
Tolerance declines because of the risk to growing points and clusters.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tolerance declines because of the risk to growing points and clusters.

items.text: Fruit Growth
Tolerance for larvae close to clusters and fruits becomes lower.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tolerance for larvae close to clusters and fruits becomes lower.

items.text: Near Harvest
The calculation changes because of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The calculation changes because of:

items: Limited capacity of an intervention to protect a crop close to picking.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Limited capacity of an intervention to protect a crop close to picking.

items.text: One threshold should therefore not be used throughout the season.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]One threshold should therefore not be used throughout the season.

items.text: Thresholds in Processing and Fresh-Market Tomatoes
Fresh Market
Limited infestation may result in complete rejection of a fruit, so tolerance is generally low.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Limited infestation may result in complete rejection of a fruit, so tolerance is generally low.

items.text: Processing
The following should be considered:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be considered:

items: Effects of damaged fruit on raw-material quality.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Effects of damaged fruit on raw-material quality.

items.text: The lower price of processing tomatoes does not mean that high infestation can be tolerated, because a high rejection percentage may make harvesting and transport uneconomic.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The lower price of processing tomatoes does not mean that high infestation can be tolerated, because a high rejection percentage may make harvesting and transport uneconomic.

items.text: Seventh: Evaluation of Treatment Efficacy
Abbott's Formula
This is used when a treatment and an untreated control are present and infestation is measured after treatment.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is used when a treatment and an untreated control are present and infestation is measured after treatment.

items.text: Corrected efficacy according to Abbott (%) = [(Survival in the control − Survival in the treatment) ÷ Survival in the control] × 100
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Corrected efficacy according to Abbott (%) = [(Survival in the control − Survival in the treatment) ÷ Survival in the control] × 100

items.text: It is suitable for experiments in which initial pest density does not differ substantially among treatments.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is suitable for experiments in which initial pest density does not differ substantially among treatments.

items.text: Henderson–Tilton Formula
This is more appropriate when pest numbers before treatment differ among treatments and the control.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is more appropriate when pest numbers before treatment differ among treatments and the control.

items.text: Corrected reduction (%) = [1 − (Pest number after treatment ÷ Pest number before treatment) × (Pest number before treatment in the control ÷ Pest number after treatment in the control)] × 100
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Corrected reduction (%) = [1 − (Pest number after treatment ÷ Pest number before treatment) × (Pest number before treatment in the control ÷ Pest number after treatment in the control)] × 100

items.text: It may be used with:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It may be used with:

items.text: The equation should not be applied mechanically when sampling units or counting methods differ among visits.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The equation should not be applied mechanically when sampling units or counting methods differ among visits.

items.text: Reduction in Fruit Infestation
Relative reduction is calculated as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Relative reduction is calculated as:

items.text: Reduction in fruit infestation (%) = [(Fruit infestation in the comparison − Fruit infestation in the programme) ÷ Fruit infestation in the comparison] × 100
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Reduction in fruit infestation (%) = [(Fruit infestation in the comparison − Fruit infestation in the programme) ÷ Fruit infestation in the comparison] × 100

items.text: The comparison should be specified as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The comparison should be specified as:

items.text: Evaluation of Speed of Action
Control tools differ in how rapidly effects appear:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Control tools differ in how rapidly effects appear:

items: Neuroactive insecticides may rapidly reduce movement and feeding.
Growth regulators require time until molting.
Bt stops feeding before complete mortality.
Fungi and viruses act relatively slowly.
Parasitoid effects appear within the generation.
Mating-disruption effects appear in mating and the following generation.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Neuroactive insecticides may rapidly reduce movement and feeding.
[[prepared]]Mating-disruption effects appear in mating and the following generation.

items.text: Assessment dates should therefore be selected according to mode of action, and all tools should not be compared after only 24 hours.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Assessment dates should therefore be selected according to [[term:mode-of-action]]mode of action[[/term]], and all tools should not be compared after only 24 hours.

items.text: Duration of Protection
A treatment should not be evaluated at only one time point.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A treatment should not be evaluated at only one time point.

items.text: Measure:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Measure:

items.text: One material may produce rapid high mortality but have a short effect, while another may provide slower but more sustained suppression.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]One material may produce rapid high mortality but have a short effect, while another may provide slower but more sustained suppression.

items.text: Eighth: Field-Evaluation Design
Treatment and Replicate
In scientific trials, the experimental unit should be clearly defined, such as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In scientific trials, the experimental unit should be clearly defined, such as:

items.text: Sufficient replication should be used to distinguish treatment effects from natural variability.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Sufficient replication should be used to distinguish treatment effects from natural variability.

items.text: A large number of plants within one plot should not be treated as independent replicates when they all receive the same treatment and experience the same conditions; this constitutes pseudoreplication.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A large number of plants within one plot should not be treated as independent replicates when they all receive the same treatment and experience the same conditions; this constitutes pseudoreplication.

items.text: Randomization
Treatments should be randomized or assigned through an appropriate design to reduce the effects of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Treatments should be randomized or assigned through an appropriate design to reduce the effects of:

items.text: Where a clear field gradient exists, a randomized complete block design may be used.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Where a clear field gradient exists, a randomized complete block design may be used.

items.text: Sampling
The sample should be:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The sample should be:

items: Distributed across edges and interior according to the design.
Collected at approximately the same time.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Distributed across edges and interior according to the design.

items.text: The following should be defined in advance:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be defined in advance:

items.text: Pretreatment Measurements
Pretreatment measurements allow:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pretreatment measurements allow:

items.text: Post-treatment measurements alone are insufficient when initial pest densities are unknown.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Post-treatment measurements alone are insufficient when initial pest densities are unknown.

items.text: Statistical Analysis
The appropriate test depends on data structure.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The appropriate test depends on data structure.

items.text: Data may require:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Data may require:

items: Binomial models for percentage fruit infestation.
Poisson or negative-binomial distributions for counts.

items.text: The mean alone should not be used without estimating variability and confidence intervals.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The mean alone should not be used without estimating variability and confidence intervals.

items.text: Ninth: Performance Dashboard
Weekly Indicator Dashboard
End-of-Season Evaluation Card
Composite Performance Index
A project may create a composite index, but it should not conceal component details.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A project may create a composite index, but it should not conceal component details.

items.text: Example weighting:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Example weighting:

items.text: Weights should be selected according to project objectives.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Weights should be selected according to project objectives.

items.text: In an export project, residue compliance may receive greater weight, whereas in processing tomatoes greater importance may be assigned to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In an export project, residue compliance may receive greater weight, whereas in processing tomatoes greater importance may be assigned to:

items.text: The composite index should not be used without presenting each component separately.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The composite index should not be used without presenting each component separately.

items.text: Analysis of Causes of Deviation
When a target is not achieved, the cause should be classified.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When a target is not achieved, the cause should be classified.

items.text: Biological Causes
Operational Causes
Chemical Causes
Agronomic Causes
Economic Causes
The cause should be identified before the following season is modified.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The cause should be identified before the following season is modified.

items.text: Comparison Among Seasons
Interpretation should account for differences in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Interpretation should account for differences in:

items.text: A lower number of sprays in a cool season does not necessarily indicate greater programme success than in a hot, high-pressure season.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A lower number of sprays in a cool season does not necessarily indicate greater programme success than in a hot, high-pressure season.

items.text: Multi-Year Programme Evaluation
Some IPM benefits do not appear within one season.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some IPM benefits do not appear within one season.

items.text: The following should preferably be tracked:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should preferably be tracked:

items: Change in date of first pest appearance.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Change in date of first pest appearance.

items.text: Establishment costs may be high in the first year and then decline as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Establishment costs may be high in the first year and then decline as:

items.text: General Per-Feddan Calculation Example
Assume the following:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Assume the following:

items: Marketable yield under the conventional programme = Y1 tonnes/feddan.
Marketable yield under IPM = Y2 tonnes/feddan.
Cost of the conventional programme = C1.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Marketable yield under the conventional programme = Y1 tonnes/feddan.

items.text: Then:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Then:

items.text: Increase in accepted yield = Y2 − Y1
Value of the increase = (Y2 − Y1) × P
Additional cost = C2 − C1
Incremental net benefit = [(Y2 − Y1) × P] − (C2 − C1)
Incremental ROI (%) = Incremental net benefit ÷ Incremental cost × 100
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Incremental ROI (%) = Incremental net benefit ÷ Incremental cost × 100

items.text: The value of reduced costs, such as avoided sprays, should also be added to benefits.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The value of reduced costs, such as avoided sprays, should also be added to benefits.

items.text: Example of Evaluating the Addition of a Biological Component
When evaluating addition of egg parasitoids to the programme, calculate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When evaluating addition of egg parasitoids to the programme, calculate:

items.text: Additional Costs
Benefits
A decline in Tuta should not be attributed to parasitoids alone when the following also changed:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A decline in Tuta should not be attributed to parasitoids alone when the following also changed:

items.text: Interpreting Lack of Profitability Despite Biological Efficacy
An IPM programme may reduce infestation substantially without producing the highest profit when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An IPM programme may reduce infestation substantially without producing the highest profit when:

items: Pest pressure is low and intensive intervention was unnecessary.
Fixed interventions are used without thresholds.
Yield in the comparison field remains high because of seasonal conditions.
Higher fruit quality does not receive a price premium.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Pest pressure is low and intensive intervention was unnecessary.
[[prepared]]Yield in the comparison field remains high because of seasonal conditions.
[[prepared]]Higher fruit quality does not receive a price premium.

items.text: This indicates a need to improve:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This indicates a need to improve:

items.text: It does not mean that IPM is scientifically invalid.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It does not mean that IPM is scientifically invalid.

items.text: Evaluating Risk Rather Than the Mean Alone
A conventional programme may produce a high mean profit while carrying greater risk of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A conventional programme may produce a high mean profit while carrying greater risk of:

items.text: An IPM programme may produce a slightly lower average profit but be more stable among seasons and less risky.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An IPM programme may produce a slightly lower average profit but be more stable among seasons and less risky.

items.text: The evaluation should therefore consider:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The evaluation should therefore consider:

items.text: Common Errors
Error One: Evaluating Success by the Number of Moths Captured
Traps are monitoring indicators, not final crop-performance measures.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Traps are monitoring indicators, not final crop-performance measures.

items.text: Error Two: Counting All Mines as Active
Old mines remain after successful control.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Old mines remain after successful control.

items.text: Error Three: Using Total Yield Alone
A large proportion may be unmarketable.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A large proportion may be unmarketable.

items.text: Error Four: Measuring Fruit Infestation by Number Without Weight
Weight loss may differ.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Weight loss may differ.

items.text: Error Five: Measuring Weight Without Counting Rejected Fruits
An infested fruit may retain its weight despite being rejected.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An infested fruit may retain its weight despite being rejected.

items.text: Error Six: Ignoring Labour Cost
Inspection, distribution, and collection have economic costs.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Inspection, distribution, and collection have economic costs.

items.text: Error Seven: Treating Every Reduction in Sprays as a Benefit
It should not be accompanied by an unacceptable increase in damage.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It should not be accompanied by an unacceptable increase in damage.

items.text: Error Eight: Comparing Fundamentally Different Fields
Differences may result from irrigation, cultivar, or soil.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Differences may result from irrigation, cultivar, or soil.

items.text: Error Nine: Using BCR Without Defining the Equation
Calculation methods differ among studies.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Calculation methods differ among studies.

items.text: Error Ten: Transferring an Economic Threshold from Another Country
Prices, costs, cultivars, and climates differ.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Prices, costs, cultivars, and climates differ.

items.text: Error Eleven: Using One Threshold Throughout the Season
Crop sensitivity changes with growth stage.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Crop sensitivity changes with growth stage.

items.text: Error Twelve: Evaluating Slow-Acting Tools Too Early
Fungi, viruses, and parasitoids require time.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Fungi, viruses, and parasitoids require time.

items.text: Error Thirteen: Ignoring Resistance and Residues
Both are real costs and risks.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Both are real costs and risks.

items.text: Error Fourteen: Assuming the Most Entomologically Effective Programme Is Automatically the Most Profitable
Its cost may exceed the value of additional benefits.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Its cost may exceed the value of additional benefits.

items.text: Error Fifteen: Changing Several Factors Simultaneously Without Documentation
This makes it difficult to identify the component responsible for the outcome.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This makes it difficult to identify the component responsible for the outcome.

items.text: Practical Priorities in Egypt
The priorities are:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The priorities are:

items: 1. Use accepted yield rather than total yield alone.
2. Measure fruit infestation by number and weight.
3. Separate Tuta losses from other causes of rejection.
4. Record the cost of every programme component.
6. Calculate the cost of protecting one accepted tonne.
7. Establish a baseline for each region and production cycle.
8. Do not transfer foreign thresholds directly.
9. Develop separate Egyptian thresholds for processing and fresh-market tomatoes.
10. Include factory and market prices in EIL calculations.
12. Evaluate programmes over two or more seasons.
13. Link residue compliance to economic viability.
14. Calculate the value of avoided sprays.
15. Treat resistance risk as a future cost.
17. Use partial budgeting when modifying one component.
18. Review causes of deviation after the season.
19. Convert field records into an analyzable database.
20. Update calculations as crop and input prices change.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. Use accepted yield rather than total yield alone.
[[prepared]]2. Measure fruit infestation by number and weight.
[[prepared]]3. Separate Tuta losses from other causes of rejection.
[[prepared]]4. Record the cost of every programme component.
[[prepared]]6. Calculate the cost of protecting one accepted tonne.
[[prepared]]7. Establish a baseline for each region and production cycle.
[[prepared]]8. Do not transfer foreign thresholds directly.
[[prepared]]9. Develop separate Egyptian thresholds for processing and fresh-market tomatoes.
[[prepared]]10. Include factory and market prices in EIL calculations.
[[prepared]]12. Evaluate programmes over two or more seasons.
[[prepared]]13. Link residue compliance to economic viability.
[[prepared]]14. Calculate the value of avoided sprays.
[[prepared]]15. Treat resistance risk as a future cost.
[[prepared]]17. Use partial budgeting when modifying one component.
[[prepared]]18. Review causes of deviation after the season.
[[prepared]]19. Convert field records into an analyzable database.
[[prepared]]20. Update calculations as crop and input prices change.

items.text: Scientific Summary of Section Fifteen
An integrated management programme for Tuta absoluta should be evaluated through a multidimensional framework that includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An integrated management programme for Tuta absoluta should be evaluated through a multidimensional framework that includes:

items.text: A decline in pest numbers alone is insufficient to demonstrate economic viability, just as high total yield is insufficient when part of the crop is unmarketable.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A decline in pest numbers alone is insufficient to demonstrate economic viability, just as high total yield is insufficient when part of the crop is unmarketable.

items.text: The most important final indicators include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The most important final indicators include:

items.text: Active mines and live larvae should be used instead of total old mines when evaluating treatment efficacy.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Active mines and live larvae should be used instead of total old mines when evaluating treatment efficacy.

items.text: The economic injury level changes with:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The economic injury level changes with:

items.text: Published values ranging from approximately 3 to more than 6 larvae per plant represent the specific conditions under which they were calculated and should not be treated as general thresholds for Egyptian fields.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Published values ranging from approximately 3 to more than 6 larvae per plant represent the specific conditions under which they were calculated and should not be treated as general thresholds for Egyptian fields.

items.text: The number or percentage of infested and rejected fruits may be more informative than total yield weight because a small hole may cause complete fruit rejection without substantially reducing its weight.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The number or percentage of infested and rejected fruits may be more informative than total yield weight because a small hole may cause complete fruit rejection without substantially reducing its weight.

items.text: Evaluation should also determine whether the programme:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Evaluation should also determine whether the programme:

items: Prevented the field from becoming a source for the next crop.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Prevented the field from becoming a source for the next crop.

items.text: A programme is successful when it achieves the best possible balance among crop protection, profitability, and sustainability, not when it produces the lowest possible pest population at any cost.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A programme is successful when it achieves the best possible balance among crop protection, profitability, and sustainability, not when it produces the lowest possible pest population at any cost.
